programs.name: Oklahoma Medicaid SoonerCare

eligibility.income_limits: For elderly (65+): Varies by program. Nursing Home Medicaid (single applicant): under $2,982/month. Aged, Blind, and Disabled (ABD) Medicaid (single): asset-related but income limit tied to SSI levels, specifics vary. MAGI-based for general SoonerCare up to 138% FPL (~$17,796/year individual). Full table not provided in sources; varies by household size, marital status, and program (e.g., children/pregnant women higher). Contact OHCA for 2026 household table.[1][5][6]
eligibility.asset_limits: Nursing Home Medicaid (single): $2,000 countable assets. ABD Medicaid (single): $9,950 countable assets. Countable assets typically include bank accounts, stocks; exempt: primary home (under equity limits), one vehicle, personal belongings, burial plots. Spousal protections apply if married.[1][6]
other_requirements: Must be Oklahoma resident and U.S. citizen/eligible immigrant.
For ABD: Aged 65+, blind, or disabled (SSA disability determination required).
Nursing Home Level of Care (NFLOC) or functional need for ADLs/IADLs for long-term care.
Not eligible for Medicare in some categories (e.g., expansion adults).

benefits.value: Comprehensive healthcare: physician visits, prescription drugs, ER/hospital stays, long-term care (nursing home, HCBS waivers for home services like personal care, home modifications), dental/vision for some. ABD covers basic care; long-term care requires assessment. No-cost/low-cost via managed care (e.g., Aetna Better Health).[1][2][7]

methods: Online: OKDHSLive.org or oklahoma.gov/ohca (MySoonerCare)
Mail/In-person: Local DHS offices or OHCA (addresses via oklahoma.gov/okdhs)
Forms: SoonerCare application via OKDHSLive.org (no specific form number listed)
application.processing_time: Lengthy and challenging; no exact timeline specified (can take weeks/months with documentation review).[6]
application.waitlist: Possible for HCBS waivers due to limited slots; not specified for basic ABD.[1]
documents: Proof of income (pay stubs, SSI awards)

geography.regional_variations: Processing/wait times may vary by local DHS office; providers via managed care plans statewide.[6]
offices_or_providers: Local Oklahoma DHS offices; managed care: Aetna Better Health, SoonerSelect plans

gotchas: Separate rules for ABD vs. Nursing Home vs. HCBS; ABD has higher asset limit but basic coverage only unless LTC assessed.
Must apply for SSA disability if not already determined.
Spousal impoverishment rules protect community spouse assets/income.
HCBS waivers have waitlists and NFLOC requirement.
Income slightly over limits? Apply anyway for possible qualification.
Documentation-heavy; incomplete apps delay processing.
programs.data_shape_notes: Eligibility splits by category (ABD, Nursing Home, HCBS) with different income/asset limits; functional assessments determine LTC benefits; MAGI for non-LTC, strict limits for LTC; varies by marital status and household size.

programs.name: Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) Waivers (ADvantage Waiver)

eligibility.age: 65+ or 19-64 with physical disability (no intellectual disability or cognitive impairment related to developmental disability)
eligibility.income_limits: Equivalent to 300% of Federal Benefit Rate (FBR); in 2025, up to $2,901/month per applicant regardless of marital status (each spouse individually if both applying). Older sources cite $2,199/month, but 2025 figure is $2,901[2]. Must qualify financially for SoonerCare Medicaid[3][4][5].
eligibility.asset_limits: Resource limit of $2,000 (countable assets). Home equity limit of $730,000 if applicant lives in home or has intent to return (2025). Exemptions: primary home if equity under limit or if spouse/dependent child lives there[2][3].
other_requirements: Oklahoma resident and U.S. citizen/qualified immigrant[1]
Nursing Facility Level of Care (NFLOC) via Uniform Comprehensive Assessment Tool (UCAT) III; physical impairment affecting ADLs/IADLs[1][2][3]
At risk of nursing home placement; needs cannot be met without waiver services[2][5]
Reside in own home or family member's home[4]
Available waiver slot (limited enrollment)[3][6]
Not solely for Medicaid eligibility[6]

benefits.value: Home and community-based services as alternative to nursing facility placement, including those needed at least monthly to avoid institutionalization (specific services determined by individualized plan via case manager; promotes independence, choice, self-direction for frail elderly/disabled adults)[1][5][6]. Exact services not listed in sources but tailored to NFLOC needs.

methods: Call Oklahoma Human Services (OKDHS) or visit local county OKDHS office for assessment[3]
Online: Oklahoma Human Services website (general application start)[1]
Phone interview by Adult and Family Services specialist for financial eligibility; nurse assessment (phone or in-person) for medical[1][3]
application.processing_time: Not specified; includes financial interview, nurse assessment, case manager plan development[1][3]. Annual redetermination[3].
application.waitlist: Yes; limited slots. If full, placed on waiting list until slot opens[3][6].
forms: No specific form name/number listed; starts with OKDHS assessment and UCAT III for NFLOC[2][3]
documents: Income/resource proof for SoonerCare financial eligibility; medical/functional info for nurse assessment (prepared via interview/home visit)[1][3]

geography.regional_variations: Apply via local county OKDHS office; potential waitlist variation by demand, but not specified[3]. Providers may differ regionally[4].
offices_or_providers: Local county OKDHS offices; example provider contact: 918-477-9080 (regional)[4]

gotchas: Limited waiver slots lead to waitlists; not guaranteed even if eligible[3][6]
Developmentally disabled 19-64 must lack intellectual disability/cognitive impairment[1][2][4][5][6]
Must need specific waiver services monthly to avoid institutionalization; not just for Medicaid[6]
Income/asset limits updated annually (e.g., 2025: $2,901 income, $730k home equity)[2]
Disabled participants can continue post-65, but age 65+ now includes those with IDD[2][5]
programs.data_shape_notes: Limited enrollment with statewide waitlist; tiered by age/disability type (65+ frail, 19-64 physical/DD without cognitive issues); financial tied to SoonerCare with annual FBR-based updates; NFLOC via UCAT III assessment

programs.name: Programs of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)

eligibility.income_limits: Income must be equal to or less than 300% of the SSI Federal Benefit Rate (consistent with Oklahoma's HCBS programs like ADvantage Waiver). As of 2025, this is $2,901 per month for an individual; exact amounts may adjust annually with SSI rates and do not explicitly vary by household size in PACE documentation, though Medicaid long-term care rules apply[3][5].
eligibility.asset_limits: Assets valued at $2,000 or less for an individual (excluding primary home), per Medicaid long-term care eligibility standards applied to PACE[5]. What counts: countable assets like bank accounts, investments; exempt: primary home, one vehicle, personal belongings, burial plots.
other_requirements: Live in a PACE service area (not statewide; limited to specific providers' regions)[1][2][3][4]
Meet nursing facility level of care, determined by state via UCAT assessment[1][3]
Able to live safely in the community as assessed by PACE interdisciplinary team (IDT)[1][2][3]
Agree to use PACE provider and contractors as sole service providers (except emergencies)[1]

benefits.value: Comprehensive all-inclusive services including primary care, hospital/inpatient care, emergency care, prescription drugs, social services, restorative therapies, nutritional counseling, transportation, personal care, adult day health center (with hot breakfast/lunch on center days), home care, and activities; PACE becomes sole source for Medicare/Medicaid-covered services[1][3][7][9]. No specific dollar amounts or hours per week stated; services tailored by IDT.

methods: Email: PACEInquiry@okhca.org[3]
Contact PACE provider intake staff (e.g., LIFE PACE or Valir PACE) to initiate; they aid with OKDHS financial eligibility[1][2][4]
application.processing_time: Not specified; new medical level of care determination required if prior UCAT >6 months old[1]
application.waitlist: Not mentioned in sources; may vary by provider/service area
forms: UCAT assessment (completed by PACE or OHS nurse)[1]
OKDHS financial eligibility application (initiated via PACE intake)[1]
documents: Proof of age, residency in service area, income/asset documentation for Medicaid financial eligibility, medical records for nursing home level of care assessment[1][5]

geography.restrictions: Limited to PACE service areas of approved providers (e.g., LIFE PACE, Valir PACE in Oklahoma City/Shawnee areas)[2][4]
geography.regional_variations: Services delivered by specific PACE organizations (e.g., different adult day centers); eligibility assessments and enrollment via local providers; potential differences in wait times or capacity by site[1][2][4]
offices_or_providers: Valir PACE: Oklahoma City Adult Day Center, Shawnee Adult Day Center[4]
LIFE PACE: Defined service area (specific counties not listed)[2]

gotchas: Not available statewide—must live in a specific PACE provider's service area[2][3][4]
Must use PACE as sole provider (no other services except emergencies); financially liable for unauthorized care[1]
Nursing home level of care required, but must be safe in community with PACE support—IDT can deny if not[1][3]
Financial eligibility tied to Medicaid HCBS (300% SSI income limit), not automatic for all Medicare enrollees[3][5]
Cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage, hospice, or certain other programs[6]
programs.data_shape_notes: Limited to specific provider service areas (not statewide); financial eligibility mirrors ADvantage Waiver (300% SSI income, $2,000 assets); requires both state nursing facility certification and provider IDT safety assessment; capitated all-inclusive model via regional PACE organizations

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)

eligibility.income_limits:
Oklahoma-specific monthly income limits (likely current as of search data; verify with OKDHS for 2026 updates):
- **QMB**: $1,235 single, $1,663 married couple[3].
- **SLMB**: Above QMB up to $1,478 single, $1,992 married couple[3].
- **QI**: Above SLMB up to $1,660 single, $2,239 married couple[3].
Federal 2026 baselines are higher (QMB: $1,350 single/$1,824 couple), but states set their own; Oklahoma uses stricter limits[3][5]. Limits based on Federal Poverty Level, updated April 1 annually[1].
eligibility.asset_limits: Federal limits apply in Oklahoma: $9,090 single, $13,630 married couple for QMB, SLMB, QI. Counts typical countable resources (cash, bank accounts, stocks); exempts home, one car, personal belongings, burial plots (exact exemptions per federal Medicaid rules; confirm with OKDHS)[3].
other_requirements: Must be eligible for Medicare Part A (even if not enrolled for QMB)[1].
Must have Medicare Part A and B for SLMB and QI[5].
U.S. citizen or qualified immigrant.
Oklahoma resident.

benefits.value:
- **QMB**: Pays Medicare Part A premiums (if applicable), Part B premiums/deductible, coinsurance/deductibles for Parts A/B services[1][3].
- **SLMB**: Pays Medicare Part B premiums only[1][3].
- **QI**: Pays Medicare Part B premiums only[1][3].
Automatic qualification for Extra Help (low-income subsidy for Part D drugs)[5].

methods: In-person or phone: Local OKDHS Human Services Center (find via oklahoma.gov/okdhs or call 405-521-3646 for nearest)[4].
No specific online URL or form number listed; apply through OKDHS Medicaid agency[1][4].
Mail possible via local center.
application.processing_time: Not specified in sources; typically state Medicaid processing (contact OKDHS for current times)[1].
application.waitlist: QI has first-come, first-served with priority to prior-year recipients; limited funds may create waitlist[5].
forms: Appendix C-1 Schedules (VI for QMB, VII for SLMB, VII.a for QI); request via OKDHS[4].
documents: Proof of income (pay stubs, SSI awards, pension statements).

geography.regional_variations: Administered locally via OKDHS Human Services Centers; possible differences in processing speed by office, but no specified variations[4].
offices_or_providers: Local OKDHS Human Services Centers (statewide network)[4].

gotchas: Oklahoma uses stricter income limits than federal baselines; check state-specific amounts[3].
QI requires annual reapplication, first-come-first-served, and excludes those eligible for other Medicaid[5].
Asset test applies (unlike some states with no limit)[1][3].
Must apply to state Medicaid agency, not Medicare directly[1].
Limits update April 1; verify current via OKDHS Appendix C-1[1][4].
programs.data_shape_notes: Tiered by program (QMB/SLMB/QI) with Oklahoma-specific income caps below federal; asset limits fixed federally but applied statewide; QI funding-capped with annual renewal and priority queue.

programs.name: Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP)

eligibility.income_limits: For households with elderly (60+) or disabled members in Oklahoma (Oct 1, 2025 - Sep 30, 2026): Gross income limit of 165% FPL; Net income limit of 100% FPL. Seniors 60+ only need to meet the net income test. Examples: $15,060 net for 1 person; $20,440 net for 2 people (2025 figures). Full table example from Hunger Free Oklahoma: Base $5,867 gross for 8 persons, +$596 per additional; households with 60+ may qualify with higher gross incomes[1][2][4][5].
eligibility.asset_limits: Countable resources limited to $4,250 if household has member 60+ or disabled ($2,750 otherwise). Counts: cash, bank accounts. Exempt: home and land, most retirement/pension plans (withdrawals count as income), household goods[2].
other_requirements: U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen
Social Security number for all household members
Meet work requirements (unemployed adults ages 18-53; exemptions for 60+)
Household defined as those who buy/prepare food together
Live in Oklahoma

programs.name: Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)

eligibility.income_limits: Gross monthly income must be at or below 130% of federal poverty level: 1 person $1,695; 2 people $2,291; 3 people $2,887; 4 people $3,482; 5 people $4,079; 6 people $4,674. Everyone under the same roof sharing a utility meter counts as household, even if not sharing expenses[1][2].
eligibility.asset_limits: Eligibility based on available resources, but no specific dollar limits or exemptions detailed in sources[6].
other_requirements: Responsible for home energy payment (household ineligible if non-resident pays utility directly)[2][6].
US citizen or legal permanent resident[2].
One application per household per program component[2].
Tribal members may choose OKDHS or tribal program, but not both in same federal fiscal year[2][6].

benefits.value: Heating: $40-$500 one-time payment to utility; Cooling: $150-$650; Crisis: up to $750. Varies by income, household size, fuel type, dwelling type[1][6].

methods: Online: www.okdhslive.org or www.OKDHSLive.org[2][6][8].
Phone: (405) 522-5050 (select energy assistance; for life-threatening crisis referral)[2][6].
In-person/mail: Local Oklahoma Human Services offices (specific addresses via OKDHS)[2].
application.processing_time: Up to 60 calendar days for heating/cooling; crisis evaluated individually[2].
application.waitlist: Funding distributed by priority (e.g., elders, young children first) until exhausted; no formal waitlist mentioned[3].
forms: OKDHSLive online application; LIHEAP OKDHSLive Instructions available for download[2][8].
documents: 30 days proof of income for all under same roof (if no open benefits case)[8].
Proof of responsibility for energy bill (utility bill)[2][8].
For life-threatening crisis: note from licensed health care professional[2].

geography.restrictions: Available statewide via OKDHS; tribal members in tribe service areas may use tribal LIHEAP instead[2][4].
geography.regional_variations: Tribal programs (e.g., Wichita, Chickasaw) have separate eligibility/income guidelines and providers; cannot duplicate with OKDHS in same year[3][4][5].
offices_or_providers: Oklahoma Human Services (OKDHS) statewide; tribal programs for eligible members (e.g., Wichita Tribe (405) 247-2425)[2][3].

gotchas: Household includes all under same roof/utility meter, even non-expense sharers; must apply together[1][2].
Only one payment per component per household; tribal/OKDHS cannot both be used same fiscal year[2][6].
Not year-round: heating Dec-Feb, cooling summer, crisis/emergency only[1][2].
Application does not guarantee benefits; eligibility determined post-submission[8].
Someone else paying utility directly disqualifies household[2].
programs.data_shape_notes: Statewide via OKDHS with tribal alternatives; benefits scale by household size, income, fuel; priority to elders/children; seasonal components (heating/cooling/crisis); household defined by utility meter

programs.name: Home-Delivered Meals (Meals on Wheels)

ADvantage Waiver has limited income/resources (varies, determined by DHS caseworker).
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits for OAA program. ADvantage has resource limits assessed by DHS caseworker, but specifics not detailed; primary residence and one vehicle typically exempt in similar programs.
other_requirements: Homebound (unable to leave home without assistance; not eligible if can use taxi/friend's car or if household member can prepare meals/drive)
No one available to provide meal preparation assistance
Disability for under 60 if living with eligible 60+ participant
Spouse of participant if in best interest per AAA
For ADvantage: nursing home level of care, Medicaid eligible

methods: Contact local Area Agency on Aging (AAA) or Meals on Wheels provider (e.g., Oklahoma City: mealsonwheelsokc.org/applyforservices)
Phone varies by region (e.g., Oklahoma City Meals on Wheels association)
Complete intake form (in-person or via provider)
For ADvantage: local DHS county office caseworker

application.waitlist: Common; e.g., ~2 months in Oklahoma City due to limited capacity.
forms: Intake form (provider-specific, e.g., Meals on Wheels Oklahoma City)
documents: For ADvantage: financial/medical assessment docs via DHS

geography.restrictions: Available via local AAAs; some counties may have capacity limits.

offices_or_providers: Area Agencies on Aging statewide
Meals on Wheels Oklahoma City (Oklahoma County)
SWODA/SOCAG (southwest OK)
Local DHS for ADvantage

Homebound strictly defined—can leave with help? Likely ineligible
Under 60 generally ineligible unless living with 60+ eligible
OAA free/no income test, but ADvantage has financial/medical hurdles; private options charge ~$9/meal
Weekday only (Mon-Fri), no weekends

programs.name: Respite Voucher Programs in Oklahoma
programs.note: Oklahoma operates multiple respite voucher programs with different eligibility criteria. This guide covers the three primary programs for elderly caregivers.

programs.program_name: OAA National Family Caregiver Support Program (OAA NFCSP)

eligibility.caregiver_age: 55 or older (for grandparents raising grandchildren) OR primary caregiver of any age caring for someone 60+
eligibility.care_recipient_requirements: Needs assistance with at least two activities of daily living OR limited by Alzheimer's disease or dementia
other_requirements: Caregiver must be primary caregiver
Care recipient must be age 60 or older

benefits.value: Four (4) $100 respite vouchers per quarter for older relatives raising children; amount varies for other caregivers

methods: Contact your local Area Agency on Aging (AAA) directly

geography.restrictions: Must contact local AAA; contact information varies by region
geography.regional_variations: Administered through regional Area Agencies on Aging
geography.offices_or_providers: Multiple AAA offices statewide (contact information available through Oklahoma Department of Human Services)

gotchas: No income limit, but must meet specific care needs criteria
Requires contact with local AAA rather than state office
Program specifically targets caregivers age 55+ or those caring for seniors 60+

eligibility.caregiver_age: 18 or older (full-time unpaid caregiver) OR grandparent or other relative age 54 or younger raising a child full-time
eligibility.care_recipient_requirements: Needs assistance with activities of daily living to stay at home in the community
eligibility.income_limits: Household income does not exceed $90,000 per year
other_requirements: Care recipient must be Oklahoma resident
Does not qualify for services of other respite programs (OAA NFCSP)
Does not receive services from a program with a respite component or receive in-home services paid by Medicare, Hospice, or ADvantage
Caregiver does not receive respite services or payments from other respite programs

benefits.value: Specific dollar amounts not detailed in sources; vouchers can be used to hire respite care provider at negotiated hourly wage within funding limits

methods: Email: respite@ouhsc.edu (indicate county of residence)
Contact: Sooner SUCCESS

geography.restrictions: Must be Oklahoma resident; county of residence required for email inquiries
geography.offices_or_providers: Operated by OUHSC Sooner SUCCESS statewide

gotchas: Care recipient must be under 60 years old (different from OAA NFCSP which targets 60+)
Cannot receive respite from other programs simultaneously
Income limit of $90,000 may exclude some middle-income families
Designed for caregivers who don't qualify for other respite programs

programs.program_name: DDS Respite Voucher Program (Developmental Disabilities Services)

eligibility.care_recipient_requirements: Diagnosis of developmental disability per Section 1408 of Title 10 of Oklahoma Statutes
eligibility.income_limits: Caregiver's adjusted gross income less than $75,000
other_requirements: Caregiver must be Oklahoma resident
Caregiver must reside with and provide at least 8 hours per day of care to eligible care recipient
Care recipient does not receive services through DDS Home and Community-Based Services Waiver
Care recipient receives less than 20 hours per week of state-funded services (some exceptions apply)
If in DHS custody, care recipient must be living in kinship home
Caregiver does not receive Family Support Assistance Payment on behalf of care recipient
Caregiver must not receive respite services funded through another state or federal program

benefits.value: Specific dollar amounts not detailed; vouchers issued for use from date of issuance and valid for 90 days

methods: Email: DDS.RespiteVoucher@okdhs.org
application.processing_time: Written determination provided within 30 days of completed application receipt; applications considered in chronological order
application.waitlist: Applications processed chronologically; authorization subject to continued availability of funding
application.forms: Respite voucher application form (specific form number not provided in sources)
documents: Completed application with required attachments (specific attachments not detailed in available sources)

geography.regional_variations: Two regional contact numbers: OKC (800) 522-1064 and Tulsa (800) 522-1075
geography.offices_or_providers: Operated by Oklahoma Department of Human Services Developmental Disabilities Services

gotchas: Limited to care recipients 18 years or younger (not for elderly)
Requires 8+ hours per day of in-home care provision by caregiver
Cannot receive DHS waiver services simultaneously
Income limit of $75,000 is lower than Lifespan program
Vouchers valid only 90 days from issuance
Applications valid only for state fiscal year (July 1-June 30); must reapply annually
Incomplete applications returned for correction; this delays processing

respite_care_provider_requirements.residency: Must live outside of caregiver and care recipient's home
respite_care_provider_requirements.documentation: Must have Social Security number and Social Security card
respite_care_provider_requirements.who_can_provide: Can be family member, friend, church member, or anyone meeting above criteria

where_care_can_be_provided: Center-based program (adult day services center, nursing facility, etc.)
Any location agreed to by caregiver and respite care provider

key_differences_between_programs.age_of_care_recipient: OAA NFCSP: 60+; Lifespan: birth-59; DDS: 18 or younger
key_differences_between_programs.primary_focus: OAA NFCSP: elderly with chronic conditions/dementia; Lifespan: younger individuals needing ADL assistance; DDS: children with developmental disabilities
key_differences_between_programs.caregiver_age_flexibility: OAA NFCSP: 55+ for grandparents; Lifespan: 18+ or 54 or younger for relatives raising children; DDS: no age requirement

programs.data_shape_notes: Oklahoma operates three distinct respite voucher programs with non-overlapping eligibility criteria based primarily on care recipient age and condition type. Families must determine which program applies to their situation. The DDS program is the only one specifically for elderly caregivers of children with developmental disabilities. Income limits vary significantly ($0 to $90,000). Processing timelines and specific benefit amounts are not fully detailed in public sources. Regional variation exists primarily in contact methods (OKC vs. Tulsa for DDS; local AAA for OAA NFCSP).
next_steps_for_families: Determine care recipient's age and primary condition (developmental disability, chronic illness/dementia, or other ADL needs)
Check income eligibility for applicable program(s)
Contact appropriate program using provided phone numbers or email
Request application materials and list of required documents
Submit completed application with all required attachments
Expect 30-day determination timeline for DDS program; timeline not specified for other programs

For elderly loved ones specifically, families should focus on the **OAA National Family Caregiver Support Program** (for seniors 60+) or the **Lifespan Respite Grant Voucher Program** (for younger individuals under 60)[2][4][7].

**Critical gaps in available information:**
- Specific dollar amounts for vouchers are not publicly detailed in these sources
- Required application documents are not fully enumerated

**Recommendation:** Contact the programs directly using the phone numbers provided to obtain current benefit amounts, complete document checklists, and current processing times, as these details may change with funding availability[5].

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)

eligibility.income_limits: Family income must not exceed 125% of the federal poverty level[4][5]. The search results do not provide specific dollar amounts by household size, as federal poverty guidelines change annually. Families should contact the program directly or consult current federal poverty guidelines to determine their exact threshold.
eligibility.asset_limits: Not specified in available search results.
other_requirements: Must be unemployed[4][5]
Must show proof of residency[1]
Must show proof of age[1]
Must show proof of income[1]

benefits.value: Subsidized, part-time paid work-based training at federal minimum wage (currently $7.25/hour as of search results)[3], averaging 20 hours per week[3][4]. Participants gain work experience, on-the-job training in computer or vocational skills, and professional job placement assistance to transition to unsubsidized employment[7]. The program does not offer permanent employment but trains participants to secure it[3].

methods: Phone: 405-521-2281 (Oklahoma Department of Human Services – Aging Services)[1]
Phone: 405-522-5050 (Program Field Representative Larry Bartels; select 1 or 2 for English/Spanish, then 4 for Community Living/Aging/Adult Protective Services)[2]
Email: Larry.Bartels@okdhs.org[2]
Website: oklahoma.gov/okdhs/services/cap/scsep.html (contains statewide map with county-specific contact information)[5]
In-person: Contact county-specific SCSEP office through statewide map on website[5]
application.processing_time: Not specified in available search results.
application.waitlist: Not specified in available search results.
application.forms: Not specified in available search results.

geography.restrictions: Program operates in 34 counties through Oklahoma Department of Human Services sub-contractors and three identified sub-contractors[2]. Specific counties served include: Blaine, Cimarron, Dewey, Ellis, Garfield, Grant, Harper, Kay, Kingfisher, Major, Noble, Texas, Woods, and Woodward (through one provider)[3]. Additional counties served by ASCOG (Association of South Central Oklahoma Governments) include: Beckham, Custer, Greer, Harmon, Jackson, Kiowa, Roger Mills, and Washita[1]. Remaining Oklahoma counties are covered by National SCSEP Grantees[2].
geography.regional_variations: Different sub-contractors serve different regions of Oklahoma[2]. Host agencies vary by county and include RSVP, senior centers, city/town offices, libraries, senior nutritional programs, historical museums, non-profit thrift stores, YWCA, sheltered workshops, Salvation Army, Workforce Oklahoma centers, United Way, VFW, Indian Tribal offices, and homeless day centers[3].
offices_or_providers: Oklahoma Department of Human Services – Aging Services (primary administrator)[1][2]
ASCOG (Association of South Central Oklahoma Governments) - sub-contractor[1]
OEDA (Oklahoma Economic Development Authority) - coordinates services in 13-county region[3]
National SCSEP Grantees cover remaining counties[2]

gotchas: This is NOT permanent employment — it is subsidized training designed as a bridge to unsubsidized work[3][6]. Families should understand participants must actively seek permanent employment.
Income limit is strict: 125% of federal poverty level, not a percentage of median income[4][5]. This is significantly lower than many other assistance programs.

Enrollment priority is given to specific groups: veterans and qualified spouses receive first priority, followed by those over 65, with disabilities, with low literacy, with limited English proficiency, in rural areas, homeless or at risk of homelessness, with poor employment prospects, or who have exhausted American Job Center services[4]. Non-priority applicants may face longer waits.
The program provides only part-time work (average 20 hours/week)[3][4], not full-time employment. This may not meet all household income needs.
Minimum wage is the only guaranteed compensation — currently $7.25/hour federally[3], though state or local minimums may be higher.
Coverage varies significantly by county — some areas have multiple providers while others are served by national grantees. Availability and responsiveness may differ.
The search results do not specify processing times, waitlist lengths, or whether slots are limited by county, which could affect access.
programs.data_shape_notes: SCSEP is a statewide program with county-level variations in providers and host agencies. Benefits are fixed (20 hours/week at minimum wage) rather than scaled. Eligibility is based on a strict income threshold (125% of federal poverty level) rather than a sliding scale. The program is administered through multiple sub-contractors and national grantees, creating potential variation in application processes and wait times by region. Critical data gaps: specific dollar income thresholds by household size, processing times, waitlist information, and formal application forms are not provided in available sources.

## Key Takeaways for Families

**Start here:** Call 405-521-2281 or visit oklahoma.gov/okdhs/services/cap/scsep.html to confirm your county's provider and current income limits (which change annually based on federal poverty guidelines)[1][2].

**Income is the biggest hurdle:** At 125% of federal poverty level, this program serves only the lowest-income seniors[4][5]. A family should verify their exact threshold before investing time in the application.

**This is job training, not a job:** SCSEP provides 20 hours/week of paid training at minimum wage to help seniors re-enter the workforce, not permanent employment[3][4]. It's designed as a stepping stone to unsubsidized work.

**Veterans get priority:** If your loved one is a veteran or the spouse of a veteran, they move to the front of the line[4].

**Regional variation matters:** Different parts of Oklahoma are served by different organizations (state sub-contractors vs. national grantees), which may affect how quickly they can be served and what host agencies are available[2][3].

**Missing information:** The search results do not specify processing times, whether there are waitlists, or what formal application forms are required. Contact your county provider directly for these details.

programs.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program
ADvantage Waiver Program

eligibility.age: 65 or older, or 19-64 with physical disability (no intellectual disability or cognitive impairment), or 19-64 with developmental disability (no intellectual disability or cognitive impairment related to it), or 19-64 with progressive degenerative disease requiring prior NF/hospital care[1][2][4][5][6]
eligibility.income_limits: Must qualify financially for SoonerCare (Oklahoma Medicaid); countable monthly income limit $2,199 (subject to change); determined by OKDHS Adult and Family Services via interview; no household size table specified in sources[4]
eligibility.asset_limits: Resource limit $2,000 (subject to change); home equity interest no greater than $730,000 in 2025 if living in home or intent to return (home value minus mortgage); home exempt if spouse, minor child under 21, or permanently disabled/blind child lives there; figures subject to change[2][4]
other_requirements: Oklahoma resident and US citizen or qualified immigrant[1]
Nursing Facility Level of Care (NFLOC) via OHS Uniform Comprehensive Assessment Tool (UCAT) III; physical impairment creating barriers to ADLs/IADLs (e.g., bathing, mobility, meal prep)[1][2][4]
Reside in own home or family member's home; needs safely met with waiver services and supports[3]
Disabled by SSA or meet medical criteria; not solely for Medicaid eligibility; must need at least one waiver service monthly to avoid institutionalization[3][6]

benefits.value: Home and community-based services as alternative to nursing facility placement; specific services determined by case manager via individualized plan (e.g., to address ADLs/IADLs, promote independence); promotes choice and self-direction; no fixed dollar amounts or hours specified[1][5]

methods: Call OKDHS contact line or visit local county OKDHS office for assessment[4]
Online: Oklahoma Human Services website (oklahoma.gov/okdhs)[1][5]
Phone or in-person assessment by OKDHS nurse (medical) and social worker (financial)[1][4]
application.processing_time: Not specified; annual redetermination of eligibility[4]
application.waitlist: Limited slots; if full, placed on statewide waiting list until slot opens[4][6]
forms: No specific form name/number listed; assessment via UCAT III for NFLOC[2][4]
documents: Income/resource proof for SoonerCare financial eligibility; medical/disability documentation (e.g., SSA determination, progressive disease records); prepared via phone interview and home assessment[1][4]

geography.restrictions: None; available statewide for eligible OK residents[1][4]
geography.regional_variations: Apply via local county OKDHS offices; potential waitlist variation not detailed; providers may differ by area (e.g., CompleteOK in certain regions)[3][4]
offices_or_providers: Local county OKDHS offices; Aging Services Division[4][5]

gotchas: Limited waiver slots lead to waitlist; not guaranteed immediate access[4][6]
Developmentally disabled 19-64 excluded if intellectual disability or cognitive impairment[1][2][3][5][6]
Home equity limit $730,000 (2025); potential estate recovery on home[2]
Must need NFLOC and specific waiver services monthly; dementia alone insufficient[2][6]
Income/assets subject to change; annual redetermination required[4]

forms: Necessary forms completed at initial evaluation, including service information and client details[1]
documents: Income information may be requested voluntarily for other program referrals, but not required for eligibility[1][2]

geography.restrictions: Administered through 11 Area Agencies on Aging (AAAs) covering specific Planning Service Areas (PSAs)[2][3].
geography.regional_variations: Delivered by local AAA-contracted providers; services, wait times, and availability differ by AAA and region (e.g., ASCOG in its PSA)[1][2][3].
offices_or_providers: Network of 11 AAAs; contact local AAA via oklahoma.gov/okdhs/services/cap/older-americans-act.html[1][2][3]

gotchas: Not limited to low-income, but priority given to greatest need; may ask for income info only for other programs[1][2]
Must have IADL difficulty and live in own home[1]
Services free but donations accepted; annual reassessment required[1][2]
Apply through local AAA, not centralized state office[1][3]
programs.data_shape_notes: Administered regionally by 11 AAAs with needs-based assessment and priority tiers; no income/asset test; $150 annual materials cap

programs.name: Senior Farmers’ Market Nutrition Program (SFMNP)

eligibility.income_limits: Household income must not exceed 185% of the federal poverty income guidelines. Exact dollar amounts vary by household size and are updated annually by the USDA; families should check current guidelines at the application site (oksfmnp.org) or USDA poverty guidelines. Native Americans may qualify at age 55 in tribal programs like Choctaw or Chickasaw Nations.[1][2][3]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned in state or tribal programs.[1][2][3]
other_requirements: At least one household member must be 60+ (55+ for Native Americans in state/tribal programs).
Reside in Oklahoma and participating counties (state program).
Tribal variations: Choctaw Nation service area residents; Chickasaw citizens 55+ get priority; non-Native 60+ in Native households may qualify.
Proof of income via pay stubs (30 days), tax returns, SNAP letter, or commodities letter.
ID required; tribal card/CDIB for Natives.

benefits.value: $50 EBT debit card per eligible senior/household for fresh, locally grown fruits, vegetables, herbs, and honey at authorized farmers' markets, roadside stands (May-November). Tribal programs may provide $50-$100.[1][4][6][7][8]

methods: Online: www.oksfmnp.org (primary state method).
Phone/email: okfarmersmarkets@ouhsc.edu for questions.
Tribal: Download/print/mail/email forms from Choctaw Nation sites or visit community centers (Feb 1 start); Chickasaw Nutrition Centers (June 1 start).
In-person: Oklahoma Human Services offices, tribal community/nutrition centers, DHS for EBT issuance.
application.processing_time: Real-time status updates online; EBT card issued after approval (timeline not specified, apply early as funding depletes).[2][6]
application.waitlist: No waitlist mentioned; benefits until funding depleted (e.g., through Sept 30 or Nov 30).[1][7]
forms: Online application at oksfmnp.org; Choctaw Nation SFMNP application (downloadable).
documents: Proof of income (30-day pay stubs, tax returns, SNAP/commodities letter).
Proof of residency in service area (tribal).
SSI proof for disabled in elderly housing (Choctaw).

geography.restrictions: Participating counties only (state); tribal service areas (Choctaw 10.5 counties SE OK, Chickasaw south-central OK).
geography.regional_variations: State OK Human Services: $50 EBT, online app. Choctaw: $50, Feb-Sep, mail/email/app at centers. Chickasaw: $100 for citizens 55+/disabled/Warriors, $50 for other First Americans 55+ or non-First Americans 60+ in senior First American households; Nutrition Centers June-Oct.[1][2][4][5]
offices_or_providers: Oklahoma Human Services (statewide admin).
Choctaw Nation Community Centers.
Chickasaw Nutrition Centers (Ada, Ardmore, Duncan, Tishomingo, Purcell).

gotchas: Not fully statewide—must confirm participating counties and tribal boundaries.
Funding limited, depletes before season end (apply early Feb/June).
Tribal programs have separate eligibility/benefits—check if in Choctaw/Chickasaw area for higher $100 benefit.
EBT card only for eligible produce/honey/herbs at authorized markets—no other uses.
Native Americans qualify younger (55) but need tribal verification.
Income docs must be current; no asset test but strict 185% FPL.
programs.data_shape_notes: Tribally administered with higher benefits/earlier age in Native areas; state $50 fixed EBT, county-restricted, online-only app since 2022, funding-limited with seasonal operation.
